summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a 'synthesis reaction'?

Synthesis (combination) reactions join simpler substances into more complex products. Examples: 2Na + Cl‚āā ‚Üí 2NaCl, N‚āā + 3H‚āā ‚Üí 2NH‚āÉ. Only one product is formed.

What is a 'single displacement reaction'?

In single displacement, a free element replaces a less active element in a compound. The activity series predicts which reactions occur. Example: Fe + CuSO‚āĄ ‚Üí FeSO‚āĄ + Cu (iron is more active than copper).

What is 'reduction'?

Reduction is the gain of electrons (GER: Gain of Electrons is Reduction). The oxidation state decreases. The substance being reduced is the oxidizing agent because it accepts electrons from another species.

What is an 'oxidizing agent'?

The oxidizing agent accepts electrons from another species (is itself reduced), thereby causing the oxidation of that species. Common oxidizing agents include O‚āā, MnO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ, Cr‚āāO‚āá¬≤‚ĀĽ, and halogens.

What is a 'reducing agent'?

The reducing agent donates electrons to another species (is itself oxidized), thereby causing the reduction of that species. Common reducing agents include active metals (Na, Mg, Zn), H‚āā, and C.

A student drops a piece of copper wire into silver nitrate solution and observes silver crystals growing on the wire while the solution turns blue. Write the net ionic equation and identify what is oxidized and what is reduced.

Cu(s) ‚Üí Cu¬≤‚Āļ(aq) + 2e‚ĀĽ (oxidation, turns solution blue). 2Ag‚Āļ(aq) + 2e‚ĀĽ ‚Üí 2Ag(s) (reduction, silver crystals form). Copper is the reducing agent; silver ion is the oxidizing agent. NO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ is a spectator ion.

A water quality analyst tests a lake water sample by adding AgNO‚āÉ solution. A white precipitate forms, which darkens in light. She suspects chloride contamination. What is the precipitate, and how does this test work?

Ag‚Āļ(aq) + Cl‚ĀĽ(aq) ‚Üí AgCl(s). Silver chloride is insoluble (solubility rules: Ag‚Āļ halides are insoluble except AgF). The white precipitate darkens due to photodecomposition. This is the classic test for chloride ions.

A metallurgist extracts aluminum from bauxite ore (Al‚āāO‚āÉ) using the Hall-Heroult process, an electrolytic reduction. The overall reaction is: 2Al‚āāO‚āÉ ‚Üí 4Al + 3O‚āā. What is oxidized and what is reduced in this process?

Al goes from +3 in Al‚āāO‚āÉ to 0 in Al metal (reduction, gains 3e‚ĀĽ). O goes from -2 in Al‚āāO‚āÉ to 0 in O‚āā (oxidation, loses 2e‚ĀĽ). This process requires enormous electrical energy, making aluminum production energy-intensive.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: When zinc metal is placed in a copper sulfate solution, zinc dissolves and copper metal is deposited. This is an example of:

Zn(s) + CuSO‚āĄ(aq) ‚Üí ZnSO‚āĄ(aq) + Cu(s). Zinc is more active than copper, so it displaces copper from the solution. This is a single displacement (activity series) redox reaction.

Example: 2KClO‚āÉ ‚Üí 2KCl + 3O‚āā is an example of what type of reaction?

One compound (KClO‚āÉ) breaks down into two simpler substances (KCl and O‚āā). This is a decomposition reaction, often requiring heat, light, or electricity to initiate.

Example: What is an 'oxidizing agent'?

The oxidizing agent accepts electrons from another species (is itself reduced), thereby causing the oxidation of that species. Common oxidizing agents include O‚āā, MnO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ, Cr‚āāO‚āá¬≤‚ĀĽ, and halogens.

Example: What is a 'reducing agent'?

The reducing agent donates electrons to another species (is itself oxidized), thereby causing the reduction of that species. Common reducing agents include active metals (Na, Mg, Zn), H‚āā, and C.

Example: A pool supply store sells calcium hypochlorite (Ca(ClO)‚āā) tablets for disinfection. When dissolved, hypochlorite ion acts as an oxidizing agent: ClO‚ĀĽ + 2e‚ĀĽ + H‚āāO ‚Üí Cl‚ĀĽ + 2OH‚ĀĽ. It kills bacteria by oxidizing their cell membranes. What happens to the chlorine's oxidation state?

In ClO‚ĀĽ, Cl has an oxidation state of +1 (O is -2). In Cl‚ĀĽ, Cl is -1. Chlorine gains 2 electrons (+1 ‚Üí -1), so it is reduced. As an oxidizing agent, ClO‚ĀĽ takes electrons from bacteria cell components.
